open_items: Ratify or replace the candidate PSM1 format before stable boot ABI promotion.
Define and implement signed manifest authentication, trust anchors, algorithm agility, and revocation policy.
Persist and atomically enforce the minimum secure version across update and rollback.
Retain allocated kernel pages and install the exact r, rx, r, and rw page-table permissions.
Load and authenticate the initial-system, recovery, policy, symbols, and optional microcode artifacts.
Retain and map the finalized PBP1 container read-only only after kernel mappings and transfer state exist.
Recapture the final memory map and prove the exact ExitBootServices retry/no-later-boot-service sequence.
Transfer to PooleKernel and capture entry, panic, recovery, and reset evidence.
Qualify hostile firmware behavior, target firmware, physical hardware, and a second clean builder.
Complete Secure Boot, measured boot, TPM policy, signing, ISO, installer, and recovery gates.
claim_boundary: This contract proves bounded live UEFI filesystem discovery and exact PBC1, PSM1, and PKELF1 reads from deterministic development media.
PBC1 selects SYSTEM_A.PBM; PSM1 then selects the kernel path and binds its slot, version floor, exact file bytes, image bytes, entry contract, and SHA-256 digest.
PKELF1 segments, BSS, and relative relocations are materialized in firmware-allocated pages and checked against the independent Python oracle.
The returned r, rx, r, and rw mapping plan is validated with no writable-executable mapping, but no page tables or hardware permissions are installed.
The loaded pages remain live only while PooleBoot produces and independently transcripts a pre-exit PBP1 snapshot; no page remains resident at return and no kernel entry point is called.

The PBP1 snapshot binds the live kernel allocation, PSM1 SHA-256, virtual bounds, entry, PBC1 attempt policy and slot, normalized UEFI memory descriptors, and GOP framebuffer without signature or transfer claims.
Pre-exit PBP1 sets stack and CR3 to zero and must fail the kernel-entry profile; its logical bytes are rechecked after transcript emission, then all three temporary PBP1 pools are released.
PooleBoot closes all four opened files, frees all three file pools, all three PBP1 pools, and all kernel pages, and treats cleanup failure as fatal before returning to firmware.
No retained PBP1, installed kernel mapping, final ExitBootServices memory map, successful ExitBootServices, or transfer is claimed.
Digest equality is not manifest authentication: no signature, trust anchor, persistent rollback state, Secure Boot enforcement, measured boot, TPM use, owner signature, or trust promotion is claimed.
QEMU/OVMF evidence is a single-host non-promoting development result, not target-firmware or physical-hardware qualification.
This does not satisfy the N5 exit gate, produce a bootable ISO, authorize signing or publication, or establish production readiness.
